# Intelligent Document Processor Backend

This document outlines the implementation plan for building a .NET 8 ASP.NET Core Web API backend for an Intelligent Document Processing and RAG platform. The goal is to set up a robust, scalable backend following Clean Architecture principles, with mocked OCR and RAG services, enabling easy integration with future external services like AWS Textract and OpenAI.

## User Review Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Are there any specific naming conventions you prefer for the C# solution and projects? If not, I will use `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.sln` with projects like `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Api`, `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Application`, etc.
> - Do you already have a local PostgreSQL instance running to test this, or should we create a `docker-compose.yml` to easily spin up a PostgreSQL instance with the `pgvector` extension?

## Proposed Changes

### Project Structure & Setup

Create a new .NET 8 solution with following projects to enforce Clean Architecture:
- `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Domain` (Class Library)
- `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Application` (Class Library)
- `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Infrastructure` (Class Library)
- `IntelligentDocumentProcessor.Api` (Web API)

---

### Domain Layer

- **[NEW] src/Domain/Entities/Document.cs**: Entity for tracking document uploads (Status: Uploaded, Processing, Completed, Failed).
- **[NEW] src/Domain/Entities/DocumentChunk.cs**: Entity for storing chunks of extracted text (Will hold vector embeddings).
- **[NEW] src/Domain/Enums/DocumentStatus.cs**: Enum for the status of the Document.

---

### Application Layer

- **[NEW] src/Application/Interfaces/IDocumentRepository.cs**: Interface for standard document database operations.
- **[NEW] src/Application/Interfaces/IOcrService.cs**: Interface for OCR text extraction (returns mock text for now).
- **[NEW] src/Application/Interfaces/IEmbeddingService.cs**: Interface for embedding generation.
- **[NEW] src/Application/Interfaces/IBackgroundProcessingService.cs**: Interface for abstracting background job processing queues.
- **[NEW] src/Application/DTOs/**: Create Data Transfer Objects: `UploadDocumentDto`, `QueryDto`, `DocumentDto`, `RagResponseDto`.
- **[NEW] src/Application/Services/DocumentService.cs**: Core business logic containing:
  - Document registration
  - Calling `IBackgroundProcessingService` to defer OCR and text chunking tasks.
- **[NEW] src/Application/Services/RagService.cs**: Mock business logic for managing search/retrieval queries.

---

### Infrastructure Layer

- **[NEW] src/Infrastructure/Data/ApplicationDbContext.cs**: EF Core DbContext extending with pgvector configurations.
- **[NEW] src/Infrastructure/Repositories/DocumentRepository.cs**: Implementation using EF Core.
- **[NEW] src/Infrastructure/Services/MockOcrService.cs**: Stub for text extraction.
- **[NEW] src/Infrastructure/Services/MockEmbeddingService.cs**: Stub for vector embedding logic.
- **[NEW] src/Infrastructure/Services/MockBackgroundProcessingService.cs**: Stub for background worker queue (runs async `Task.Run()` internally to mock processing for now without real Hangfire or RabbitMQ).

---

### API Layer

- **[NEW] src/Api/Controllers/DocumentsController.cs**: Endpoints for upload (`POST /api/documents/upload`) and status check.
- **[NEW] src/Api/Controllers/RagController.cs**: Endpoint for queries (`POST /api/rag/query`).
- **[MODIFY] src/Api/Program.cs**: Dependency Injection wiring, EF Core PostgreSQL registration, and global exception handling / logging setup.

---

## Open Questions

- We need to persist the file uploads initially (locally as per requirements). Should we store these in an `Uploads` sub-directory within the `Api` project?

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
- The project will be compiling successfully.
- I will run EF Core migrations locally to ensure the database schema compiles.

### Manual Verification
- We can perform manual HTTP requests via Swagger UI generated by the Web API to test standard document upload logic, status checking, and querying.
